subject: ap-government
title: Quantitative Analysis — Ohio House Elections: Votes vs. Seats Won, 1982 and 2012
stimulus: Source: Rich Exner and James Owens, The Plain Dealer. Pie charts showing United States House of Representatives Elections for Ohio comparing Votes Won versus Seats Won for Democrats and Republicans in 1982 and 2012.

parts.prompt: Identify the party that won the highest percentage of votes and seats in 1982.
parts.scoring_notes: Only Democrats earns the point for 1982.

scoring_points.description: Identify the party that won the highest percentage of votes and seats in 1982.

required_elements: The Democrats won the highest percentage of votes and seats in 1982.
alternatives.correct_example: The Democrats won the highest percentage of votes and seats in 1982.

wrong_examples: Republicans won the most seats in 1982. (Incorrect — Republicans won fewer seats than Democrats in 1982)
Democrats won in 2012. (Answers the wrong year — 1982 is asked)
common_traps: Students often confuse 1982 and 2012 — be careful to read which year is being asked; Democrats dominated 1982 while Republicans dominated 2012
The prompt asks for the party that won the highest percentage of BOTH votes AND seats — confirm the party leads in both categories before selecting

parts.prompt: Describe a change between the percentage of seats won by Republicans in 1982 and 2012.
parts.scoring_notes: Accept any accurate description of the increase in Republican seats from 1982 to 2012. May include specific percentages.

scoring_points.description: Describe a change between the percentage of seats won by Republicans in 1982 and 2012.

required_elements: The Republicans won more seats in 2012 than in 1982.
alternatives.correct_example: The Republicans won more seats in 2012 than in 1982.

required_elements: The Republicans won 26% more seats in 2012 than in 1982.
alternatives.correct_example: The Republicans won 26% more seats in 2012 than in 1982.

wrong_examples: Republicans won fewer seats in 2012. (Incorrect — Republicans significantly gained seats between 1982 and 2012 in Ohio)
Democrats won fewer seats in 2012 than 1982. (Describes Democratic change, not Republican change as asked)
common_traps: This asks about SEATS, not votes — the Republican vote share in 2012 was roughly similar to 1982, but their seat share grew dramatically
AP readers want direction of change plus approximate magnitude — saying 'Republicans won more seats' earns the point even without exact percentages

parts.prompt: Draw a conclusion about the difference in percentage of votes won and seats won in Ohio in the 2012 election as shown in the charts.
parts.scoring_notes: The response must go beyond describing the data to drawing a conclusion about why the disparity exists (e.g., gerrymandering, redistricting). Accept any accurate inference about the cause of the gap between votes and seats.

scoring_points.description: Draw a conclusion about the difference in percentage of votes won and seats won in Ohio in the 2012 election as shown in the charts.

required_elements: While Democrats won 48% of the votes in Ohio in 2012, they only won 25% of the seats. This change could have come about due to redistricting and/or gerrymandering.
alternatives.correct_example: While Democrats won 48% of the votes in Ohio in 2012, they only won 25% of the seats. This change could have come about due to redistricting and/or gerrymandering.

wrong_examples: In 2012, Democrats won more votes than seats. (Describes the disparity without explaining its cause — does not draw a conclusion)
Republicans were more popular in Ohio in 2012. (Does not account for the fact that Democrats won nearly half the vote but only a quarter of the seats)
common_traps: A conclusion must explain WHY the disparity exists, not just restate what the data shows — gerrymandering and redistricting are the expected explanations
The data shows Democrats won roughly equal votes in 1982 and 2012 but drastically fewer seats in 2012 — the disparity between votes and seats is the key anomaly to explain

parts.prompt: Explain why the information in the charts could pose a challenge to participatory democracy.
parts.scoring_notes: The response must explain the specific connection between the data and a challenge to participatory democracy. Accept any accurate explanation that connects vote-seat disparity to reduced civic participation or political efficacy.

scoring_points.description: Explain why the information in the charts could pose a challenge to participatory democracy.

required_elements: The data, which shows that the Republicans won about the same percentage of votes but gained a disproportionate number of seats in Ohio, poses a challenge to participatory democracy, which emphasizes broad participation of constituents.
alternatives.correct_example: The data, which shows that the Republicans won about the same percentage of votes but gained a disproportionate number of seats in Ohio, poses a challenge to participatory democracy, which emphasizes broad participation of constituents.

required_elements: Political efficacy can be impacted when voters believe that their votes do not matter and that the system is rigged. Low political efficacy will lead to a narrower participation of constituents.
alternatives.correct_example: Political efficacy can be impacted when voters believe that their votes do not matter and that the system is rigged. Low political efficacy will lead to a narrower participation of constituents.

wrong_examples: People might not vote if they don't like the candidates. (Does not connect to the specific data in the charts about vote-seat disparity)
Gerrymandering is bad for democracy. (States a conclusion without explaining the mechanism connecting gerrymandered seats to challenges for participatory democracy)
common_traps: The response must tie the chart data directly to participatory democracy — explain that when votes don't translate proportionally to seats, citizens may lose confidence that their participation matters, undermining the participatory model
Define or reference 'participatory democracy' to show you understand the model: it requires broad citizen participation and responsiveness of the system to voter choices
